name: Pygeum
short_description: Pygeum is a bark extract used in men's health and prostate-support supplements, often paired with saw palmetto, nettle root, and beta-sitosterol.
overview_subtitle: A prostate-support botanical sourced from African cherry bark.
overview_content:
Pygeum is an extract from the bark of Prunus africana, also known as African cherry. It is most commonly found in men's health supplements positioned around prostate and urinary wellness.

Pygeum is often combined with saw palmetto, nettle root, pumpkin seed, zinc, or beta-sitosterol. In these formulas, pygeum is used as one part of a broader prostate-support stack rather than a standalone medical treatment.

Because urinary symptoms can be linked to conditions that require medical evaluation, pygeum should not be used to delay diagnosis or replace professional care.
interesting_fact: Pygeum comes from tree bark, so responsible sourcing and supplier transparency are especially important when comparing supplements.
how_it_works_subtitle: Pygeum is positioned around prostate and urinary wellness pathways.
how_it_works_highlight_description: Pygeum is frequently paired with saw palmetto, nettle root, and beta-sitosterol in men's health supplements.
how_it_works_content:
Pygeum bark extract contains phytosterols, triterpenes, and other plant constituents researched in relation to prostate and urinary wellness. Supplement brands usually position it around healthy urinary comfort and age-related men's wellness.

Its role in formulas is often complementary. Rather than relying on pygeum alone, many products combine it with other botanicals and minerals to support a broader prostate-health positioning.

Users should review serving size, standardization if provided, sourcing information, and the full formula before purchasing.
benefits_subtitle: Pygeum is most relevant to men's health and prostate-support formulas.

benefits_json.description: Pygeum is commonly included in supplements designed for prostate wellness.

benefits_json.description: Many formulas position pygeum around urinary comfort and routine support.

benefits_json.description: It is often paired with saw palmetto, nettle root, beta-sitosterol, pumpkin seed, and zinc.

dosage_subtitle: Serving size depends on extract concentration and formula type.
typical_dose: Follow label directions; pygeum is commonly provided as a capsule extract in prostate-support formulas.
dosage: Use only the label-directed serving and consult a healthcare professional for urinary or prostate symptoms.
dosage_content:
Pygeum dosage varies by extract strength, standardization, and whether it is used alone or in a combination supplement. Labels may list bark extract, extract ratio, or standardized phytosterol content.

People with prostate symptoms, urinary concerns, medication use, or diagnosed conditions should seek medical guidance rather than self-managing with supplements.
safety_subtitle: Pygeum is generally used in adult men's supplements, but symptom evaluation matters.

side_effects_json.title: Digestive Discomfort
side_effects_json.description: Some users may experience nausea, stomach upset, or loose stools.

side_effects_json.title: Headache
side_effects_json.description: Headache has been reported by some supplement users.

side_effects_json.title: Allergy Awareness
side_effects_json.description: Stop use if signs of sensitivity or allergic reaction occur.

side_effects_json.title: Medical Evaluation Reminder
side_effects_json.description: Urinary or prostate symptoms should be evaluated by a healthcare professional.

drug_interactions_json: Review use with a healthcare professional if taking medications for prostate or urinary conditions.
Use caution when combining multiple men's health supplements.
Discuss use before surgery or if managing chronic medical conditions.
Ask a clinician before combining with hormone-related medications or therapies.
who_should_avoid_json: People with unexplained urinary symptoms who have not been medically evaluated.
People taking prostate, urinary, or hormone-related medications without professional guidance.
People with known sensitivity to pygeum or bark-derived botanical extracts.
People who are pregnant or nursing; pygeum is generally not intended for this use.
Children unless specifically directed by a qualified healthcare professional.
faq_subtitle: Common questions about pygeum and prostate-support supplements.

faq_json.question: What is pygeum used for?
faq_json.answer: Pygeum is commonly used in men's health supplements positioned around prostate and urinary wellness.

faq_json.question: What plant does pygeum come from?
faq_json.answer: Pygeum is sourced from the bark of Prunus africana, also known as African cherry.

faq_json.question: Is pygeum the same as saw palmetto?
faq_json.answer: No. They are different botanicals, but they often appear together in prostate-support formulas.

faq_json.question: Can pygeum treat prostate disease?
faq_json.answer: No. Pygeum supplements are not treatments and should not replace medical evaluation or prescribed care.

faq_json.question: What should I compare on a pygeum label?
faq_json.answer: Compare serving size, bark extract amount, standardization if listed, sourcing transparency, and companion ingredients.

faq_json.question: Can pygeum help urinary comfort?
faq_json.answer: It is commonly positioned around urinary wellness, but symptoms should be medically evaluated.

faq_json.question: Is pygeum usually taken alone?
faq_json.answer: Many products combine pygeum with saw palmetto, nettle root, beta-sitosterol, zinc, or pumpkin seed.

faq_json.question: Who should avoid pygeum?
faq_json.answer: People with unexplained urinary symptoms, medication use, or diagnosed prostate conditions should avoid unsupervised use.

faq_json.question: Does pygeum have side effects?
faq_json.answer: Possible side effects include stomach upset, nausea, headache, or allergic reaction.

faq_json.question: Can pygeum replace medical care?
faq_json.answer: No. It should not replace professional diagnosis, treatment, or ongoing care.
